title: EU AI Act Omnibus deal closes, delaying high-risk obligations to 2027 and 2028
subtitle: After a collapsed trilogue in late April, Parliament and Council reached a provisional agreement on May 7 that pushes the bulk of the AI Act's high-risk rules past their August 2026 trigger date.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: In the early hours of May 7, the European Parliament and the Council reached a provisional agreement on the Digital Omnibus on AI. The deal replaces the AI Act's existing high-risk timelines with December 2, 2027 for Annex III systems — biometrics, employment, education, law enforcement, critical infrastructure, border management — and August 2, 2028 for Annex I, which covers AI embedded in products under existing EU sectoral safety law. Both institutions have signaled they intend to formally adopt the deal before August 2, 2026, the date the original high-risk obligations would otherwise have applied.

The agreement matters because the AI Act's high-risk regime is the largest concrete compliance burden in any AI law passed to date — conformity assessments, post-market monitoring, fundamental-rights impact assessments, registration in the EU database. The first trilogue collapsed on April 28 over the conformity-assessment architecture for products under Annex I, not over the headline delay. That technical sticking point was resolved in the May 7 round.

This caps a year of pressure on Brussels from European industry, US tech companies, and member states arguing the original timeline was unworkable given how fast model capabilities and deployment patterns are shifting. The delay is narrower than some industry groups wanted — the AI Act's general-purpose model rules, which apply to frontier providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and Mistral, are not affected. The split signals that the EU is willing to slow operational rules for deployers while keeping its grip on frontier model providers.

Takeaway for learners: regulation timelines are now part of the AI product roadmap, not a footnote. If you're building or deploying high-risk AI in Europe, the new dates buy you time but don't change the substance — the conformity assessments, the documentation, the human-oversight requirements still arrive. Use the runway, don't ignore it.
